data.title: Constellation Energy reaches deal with DoJ for Calpine acquisition
data.description:
Constellation Energy (CEG) closed -2.4% on Friday after saying it reached a resolution with the U.S. Department of Justice on the conditions required to complete its proposed $16.4 billion acquisition of Calpine, marking the final regulatory clearance needed to complete the transaction.

In July, the companies offered to divest four of Calpine's power plants in Pennsylvania and Delaware - Hay Road, Edge Moor, Bethlehem, and York 1 - as part of a separate agreement with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission.

As part of the DoJ resolution, Constellation (CEG) also agreed to divest the 828 MW York 2 natural gas-fired plant in Pennsylvania, the 605 MW gas-fired Jack Fusco Energy Center near Houston, and a minority ownership interest in the 385 MW gas-fired Gregory Power Plant near Corpus Christi, Texas.

The DoJ said the agreement addresses concerns that the acquisition could harm competition and increase prices for consumers in the ERCOT and PJM Interconnection grids.

data.title: Republican Sen. Cassidy tells CDC not to adopt hepatitis b vaccine change
data.description:
Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.), the chairman of the Senate Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions, has taken the unusual step of asking the CDC's acting director to not endorse a recommendation to change the hepatitis b vaccine schedule approved earlier Friday by a panel of advisors to the CDC.

"Acting CDC Director [Jim] O’Neill should not sign these new recommendations and instead retain the current, evidence-based approach," the lawmaker wrote in an X post.

Late Friday morning, the CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices voted 8-3 to recommend that the parents of newborns whose mothers test negative for the virus should discuss with their health care provider about whether their child should get the vaccine through shared decision-making. Mothers who decide to skip the birth dose should wait at least two months should they decide to have their child receive the series of shots later on.

The recommendation that all children, regardless of their mother's virus status, start receiving the three-dose hepatitis b vaccine series shortly after birth has been in place since 1991.

Cassidy, a medical doctor by training, said that the change was a "mistake," adding, "The birth dose is a recommendation, NOT a mandate."

The senator cited a statistic that prior to the recommendation, 20,000 newborns a year were infected with hepatitis B. Today, it's less than 20. "Ending the recommendation for newborns makes it more likely the number of cases will begin to increase again. This makes America sicker."

Cassidy has been critical of HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., despite voting to confirm him earlier this year. During his swearing-in ceremony, RFK Jr. said that he wouldn't do anything to make it more difficult or discourage individuals to get vaccinated. 

In June, RFK Jr. fired all the existing members of ACIP replacing them with his own selections. 

During an appearance before the Senate Finance Committee in September, the secretary was castigated by several Republican senators, including Cassidy, who criticized RFK Jr. for naming to ACIP individuals who served as expert witnesses in trials brought against vaccine manufacturers, which would appear to be a conflict of interest.

O'Neill, who will ultimately make the final decision on whether the hepatitis b vaccine change is implemented, also works closely with RFK Jr. as HHS deputy secretary.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Meta buys AI-wearable startup Limitless
data.description:
Meta (META) has acquired AI startup Limitless, advancing Meta’s (META) efforts to develop AI-enabled wearables and bring “personal superintelligence to everyone.”

Terms of the acquisition were not disclosed.

Limitless developed a voice recorder pendant to  be used to record interviews, conversations, lectures, and training sessions, which can then turn those conversations into AI-generated summaries.

“Meta (META) recently announced a new vision to bring personal superintelligence to everyone and a key part of that vision is building incredible AI-enabled wearables,” Limitless co-founder and CEO Dan Siroker said on the company’s webpage.

As part of its acquisition, Limitless will continue to support existing Pendant customers for at least another year but will no longer sell the Pendant to new customers. Existing customers will get the Pendant Unlimited Plan for free.

Other than the Limitless Pendant, other wearable AI devices include the Bee Pioneer Edition, Plaud NotePin, and Omi, some of which are sold without a monthly subscription fee.

Building on the success of its Ray-Ban smartglasses, Meta's (META) Limitless acquisition seems aimed to achieve the same level of ubiquity with AI wearables as smartphones and health monitoring rings.

data.title: Global Mofy AI announces $4.8M private placement of Class A shares
data.description:
• Global Mofy AI (GMM) entered into securities purchase agreements for a $4.8M private placement of its Class A ordinary shares.
  • Investors will purchase 15M Class A shares at a price of $0.31875 per share.
  • The company plans to use the net proceeds for general corporate purposes, including working capital, product development, and expansion of its AI technology platforms.

data.title: Sportsman’s Warehouse anticipates full-year EBITDA of $22M-$26M amid higher promotions and cautious Q4 outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sportsman's Warehouse Holdings, Inc. (SPWH) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Paul Stone, President and CEO, opened by highlighting, "Despite a tough consumer environment and the impact of prolonged government shutdown, we delivered our third consecutive quarter of positive same-store sales growth. Same-store sales grew 2.2% versus last year, with broad-based strength in our core categories of hunting and shooting sports as well as fishing."
  • Stone emphasized, "Our firearms business once again outperformed adjusted NICS checks, extended our market share gains for yet another quarter. While adjusting NICS checks declined, our farm unit sales increased, despite the election-driven headwinds from Q3 last year."
  • Growth drivers included a 5% increase in hunting and shooting sports, 14% in fishing, and 1.5% in apparel, while camping sales declined. E-commerce rose 8%. Stone explained, "We drove meaningful growth across several strategically important departments...Fishing delivered exceptional growth of 14%, reflecting broad participation in the category and strong execution from our teams."
  • A new store opened in Arizona, featuring a personal protection-focused format, was described as "the first of its kind in our fleet designed to meet the needs of the customers seeking both lethal and nonlegal solutions."
  • Stone cautioned, "Starting in mid-October, we started to see a slowdown in our positive sales trend, which we believe was partially driven by external disruptions from a prolonged government shutdown impacting consumer confidence. This has made for a challenging start to Q4, and while still early in the quarter, we believe it's prudent to take a conservative approach to the balance of the year."
  • CFO Jennifer Fall Jung stated, "Net sales for the quarter were $331.3 million, an increase of 2.2% compared to the prior year. We are pleased to report that the company achieved 3 consecutive quarters of year-over-year comp store sales growth."
  • Jung added, "Gross margin for the quarter was 32.8%, a 100 basis point improvement versus Q3 last year. This increase was largely driven by improved overall product margins from healthier inventory, lower freight expense due to lower inventory receipts, improved shrink and a higher penetration of sales from our fishing department, which has a higher overall gross margin."

Outlook

  • Jung revised guidance due to macroeconomic headwinds, stating, "For the full fiscal year 2025, we are adjusting our net sales range to be flat to up slightly. Furthermore, we are adjusting our full year EBITDA guidance due to margin pressure from the very promotional Q4 and lower-than-anticipated Q4 sales. We now expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $22 million to $26 million."
  • Inventory is expected to end the year at less than $330 million, and capital expenditures are projected to be less than $25 million.
  • For 2026, management anticipates "continued progress around our strategic initiatives with very modest top line growth and are focused on improved profitability through disciplined cost management, inventory efficiency and improved gross margins."

Financial Results

  • Third quarter net sales were $331.3 million, up 2.2% year-over-year.
  • Gross margin improved to 32.8%. SG&A expenses were $104.5 million or 31.5% of net sales, including about $3 million in nonrecurring add-back expenses.
  • Net income was $8,000 or $0.00 per diluted share. Adjusted net income was $3 million or $0.08 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter was $18.6 million, a 13% increase from the previous year.
  • Total inventory at the end of Q3 was $424 million, down $14.1 million year-over-year and $20 million from Q2.
  • The company paid down $13.2 million in debt during the quarter, ending with $181.9 million in total debt and $111.9 million in liquidity.

Q&A

  • Ryan Sigdahl, Craig-Hallum Capital: Asked about recent sales trends post-government shutdown and Black Friday. Jung responded, "As we spoke about in our guidance, what we saw in the end of October, where our trajectory turned more negative. We started to see that through November as well. So we didn't necessarily see a pickup from right after the government shutdown."
  • Sigdahl asked about gross margin and promotions. Jung explained, "It's a little bit of using the inventory we have to drive sales and to drive foot traffic into the store, but it's also inventory management...We don't want to carry aged inventory into 2026."
  • Anna Glaessgen, B. Riley Securities: Asked about marketing spend efficiency. Jung said, "It's really for us to be competitive in the marketplace, we feel we need to spend...We've turned more to a digital marketing and e-mail, but that's really kind of what's been working for us."
  • Mark Smith, Lake Street: Inquired about promotional intensity and inventory by category. Jung noted, "All of the categories that were down in the quarter, they actually have inventory that is more down...We are going to be leaning on the hunt and shoot category to drive sales because that's what we know drives our customers to our store."
  • Joseph Schneider, ROTH Capital (for Matt Koranda): Asked about levers to return to positive comps in hunt and shoot. Jung clarified, "Our Q3 comp was a positive too...we are not leaning strictly on firearms and animals. That's more of a layer on."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts struck a neutral to slightly negative tone, with repeated questions on the persistence of sales slowdowns, promotional margins, and inventory risks.
  • Management maintained a cautious but confident stance during prepared remarks, but became more defensive and explanatory in Q&A, especially regarding Q4 trends and promotional activity. Jung frequently explained, "We know we have an area of opportunity there and from an assortment standpoint."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism increased, with more focus on promotional dependency and macro headwinds. Management’s confidence remained but was tempered by repeated references to external pressures and conservative guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was revised downward, with EBITDA guidance lowered from $33-$45 million to $22-$26 million, citing increased promotional pressure and lower Q4 sales expectations.
  • Strategic focus remained on hunting, shooting sports, fishing, and personal protection, with more explicit caution about consumer headwinds and government shutdown impacts.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on the sustainability of sales trends, effectiveness of promotions, and inventory risk, compared to prior quarter’s focus on category growth and margin recovery.
  • Management’s tone shifted from confident and optimistic in Q2 to more reserved and explicitly cautious in Q3, emphasizing prudence and inventory discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted macroeconomic headwinds, a pressured U.S. consumer, and the impact of a prolonged government shutdown as persistent challenges.
  • Increased promotional activity to move inventory is pressuring margins.
  • Camping remains a challenged category, with sales and inventory both down, and management identified this as an area requiring further refinement.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the risk of aged inventory, margin erosion from promotions, and the effectiveness of increased digital marketing.

Final Takeaway

Sportsman’s Warehouse closed Q3 with positive same-store sales growth and strong performance in core categories such as hunting, shooting sports, and fishing, but faces mounting macroeconomic headwinds and a highly promotional retail environment. Management lowered full-year EBITDA guidance and is taking a conservative approach to Q4, prioritizing inventory discipline and cost control while leaning on core categories and digital marketing to drive traffic. The company remains focused on reducing debt, improving free cash flow, and positioning for modest growth and profitability improvements in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zumiez anticipates Q4 EPS of $0.97–$1.07 with 4%–6% sales growth as North America momentum continues
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zumiez Inc. (ZUMZ) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard Brooks opened the call by highlighting that "comparable sales grew 7.6% on top of a 7.5% increase in the year ago quarter, representing our sixth consecutive quarter of positive comparable sales growth." Brooks pointed to North America as the primary driver, with comps accelerating to double digits and a strong start to the holiday season. He emphasized, "Momentum from introducing over 100 new and emerging brands annually has carried forward into 2025...Private label performance remains a standout success story, continuing to reach new heights and representing our highest penetration levels in company history."
  • Brooks reiterated Zumiez's three main strategic priorities: driving revenue growth through customer-focused initiatives, sustaining profitability optimization, and funding strategic expansion from a strong financial foundation. He stated, "Within North America, our premium pricing strategies continue to support both margin expansion and market share growth."
  • CFO Christopher Work reported, "Third quarter net sales were $239.1 million, up 7.5% from $222.5 million in the third quarter of 2024...Comparable sales for North America were up 10%, marking the seventh consecutive quarter of comparable sales growth in the region." Work detailed, "Earnings per share reached $0.55 in the quarter, well above the high end of our guidance of $0.29."

Outlook

  • Zumiez expects Q4 net sales between $291 million and $296 million, representing sales growth of 4% to 6%. Total comparable sales are planned in the 2.5% to 4% range, with North America comps in the 4.5% to 6.5% range.
  • Operating income for Q4 is projected to be between 8% and 8.5% of sales, with anticipated earnings per share between $0.97 and $1.07 compared to $0.78 in the prior year.
  • For full year 2025, management expects year-over-year total sales growth between 4.5% and 5% and earnings per share between $0.57 and $0.67 compared to a loss of $0.09 in 2024. Product margin growth is expected to be 40 to 50 basis points for the year, and capital expenditures are planned between $10 million and $12 million.
  • Work noted, "We anticipate 40 to 50 basis points of growth in product margin in 2025 on top of 70 basis points of improvement in fiscal 2024."

Financial Results

  • Gross profit for the third quarter was $89.8 million, up 14.7% compared to $78.3 million in the prior year. Gross profit as a percentage of sales was 37.6% versus 35.2% last year, with the increase driven by leverage in store occupancy, improved product margin, and lower inventory shrinkage.
  • SG&A expense was $78 million or 32.7% of net sales, down from 34.1% last year. Operating income was $11.8 million or 4.9% of net sales, up from $2.4 million or 1.1% of net sales last year.
  • Net income for the third quarter was $9.2 million or $0.55 per share, compared to $1.2 million or $0.06 per share for the third quarter of 2024. This result includes a one-time tax item increasing diluted EPS by approximately $0.09.
  • Cash and current marketable securities stood at $104.5 million as of November 1, 2025. Inventory was $180.7 million, down 3.5% year over year.
  • The company repurchased 300,000 shares in the quarter, totaling $5.4 million.

Q&A

  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport Research Partners, asked about hard goods performance. Brooks responded, "The driver here to be clear is skate. And it is true across our global regions here in North America as well as improvements in Europe and Australia, too...after 4 painful years of massive declines in skate hard goods, this is the long-awaited turn that we've been looking for."
  • Kummetz inquired about Q4 comp assumptions. Work answered, "We are assuming on the North America side, that it will just be a little bit softer than what we saw here in November...So we are planning just a slight deceleration from November for North America."
  • Kummetz asked about private label penetration. Work stated, "It's up just right around 200 basis points year-over-year to date...Year-to-date, we're running right just under 31% of total product."
  • Jeff Van Sinderen, B. Riley Securities, probed on market share and customer demographics. Brooks explained, "We are laser-focused on our core customer...our focus is on our core consumer."
  • Van Sinderen also asked about store traffic. Work clarified, "We were up in North America, and we were down slightly in Europe...We did see a transaction gain in North America."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of hard goods growth, private label penetration, and market share drivers, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions were factual and probed for more detail rather than expressing skepticism.
  • Management remained confident and optimistic, using phrases like "we are increasingly confident" and "I'm increasingly confident in our ability to generate value for all of our stakeholders." Tone during Q&A was consistent with prepared remarks and not defensive.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more upbeat, emphasizing the turn in skate and stronger private label progress, while analysts maintained a neutral stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q4 was raised, with projected EPS and sales targets higher than those implied in Q2. Q3 comparable sales growth and profit margin expansion were both stronger than the previous quarter’s.
  • Strategic focus on private label and trend-driven merchandising intensified, with penetration up to just under 31%, compared to 30% last quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on sustainability of growth and category performance, but current quarter dialogue highlighted a more positive turn in hard goods and continued recovery in skate.
  • Management’s confidence increased, especially around North American momentum and private label success, while maintaining a cautious stance on international headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic volatility, evolving trade relationships, and global instability as key external risks.
  • Brooks noted, "Despite operating in an environment characterized by economic volatility, evolving trade relationships and global instability in certain regions, I'm increasingly confident in our ability to generate value."
  • CFO Work referenced uncertainty in the macro environment and potential impacts from tariffs, which could limit consumer spending.
  • International operations, particularly in Europe, remain challenged, with expectations for low single-digit declines in comparable sales in the region.

Final Takeaway

Zumiez management highlighted a strong third quarter driven by North American sales acceleration, private label growth, and a notable turnaround in skate hard goods. The company raised full-year and Q4 guidance, expecting continued momentum but remaining cautious given macroeconomic uncertainties and international headwinds. Executives emphasized strategic priorities of trend-driven merchandising, premium pricing, and disciplined expense management as the foundation for ongoing growth and profitability.

data.title: FCPT acquires a Baptist Health property for $4.7M
data.description:
• Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) announced on Friday the acquisition of a Baptist Health property for $4.7M. 
  • The property is newly constructed and located in a strong retail corridor in Alabama and is corporate-operated under a long-term, net lease with approximately ten years of term remaining. 
  • The transaction was priced at a 6.8% cap rate on rent as of the closing date and exclusive of transaction costs.

data.title: Quipt Home Medical holder urgers board to pursue a sale
data.description:
A Quipt Home Medical (QIPT) shareholder is urging the company to pursue a sale after it rebuffed inbound interest.

Bradley L. Radoff, who collectively with his affiliates owns approximately 4.9% of the outstanding common shares, wants the company to pursue a sale, according to a statement on Friday.

“The Board has spent years destroying its credibility by presiding over poor execution and significant shareholder value destruction," Radoff said in the statement. "To make matters worse, after apparently receiving credible offers from an interested party earlier this year, the Board chose to dilute shareholders by awarding insiders approximately 6% of the Company. 

The sale push comes after the company in August received an offer  from shareholder Forager Capital to acquire all outstanding shares at $3.10/share in cash.

Radoff said he plans to vote against every incumbent director at 2026 annual meeting if the status quo remains.

data.title: U.S. total rig count gains 5 in latest Baker Hughes survey
data.description:
The number of oil and gas drilling rigs in the U.S. gained 5 to 549 during the past week, after tumbling by 10 in the previous week, Baker Hughes said Friday in its latest survey.

In the week ended December 5, drilling rigs targeting crude oil in the U.S. rose by 6 to 413, while gas rigs declined by 1 to 129 and 7 rigs remained classified as miscellaneous.

The total count was down 40, or 6.8%, from the same time last year.

Rigs targeting oil in the Permian Basin, Williston Basin and Eagle Ford all stayed flat on the week, at 251, 29 and 26, respectively.

ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: Meihua International Medical Technologies receives Nasdaq notice
data.description:
• Meihua International Medical Technologies (MHUA) announced  on Friday that it had received a letter from the Nasdaq on December 2, 2025, notifying it that the Nasdaq staff had determined to delist the company's ordinary shares because the company failed to regain compliance with the Nasdaq Listing Rule 5550(a)(2).
  • Additionally, on December 1, 2025, the Nasdaq staff notified the company that it no longer complies with Listing Rule 5550(a)(4), which requires Nasdaq-listed companies to have at least 500,000 publicly held shares. 
  • The trading of the company’s ordinary shares will be suspended at the opening of business on December 9, 2025.
  • The company submitted the hearing request on December 4, 2025.
